preFix.title: Finger-weaving a chevron band
preFix.subtitle: Weaving a 3 cm wide herringbone-chevron band in 4-ply cotton using only the fingers, no loom needed
preFix.excerpt: Finger weaving is a pre-loom tradition practiced by many Indigenous North American, Andean, and Scandinavian peoples. The warp threads are also the weft: there is no separate weft yarn. An 8-thread setup in two contrasting colours produces a clear herringbone chevron stripe.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from public-domain finger weaving references and traditional band weaving documentation.

content.text: Finger weaving needs no loom. The threads are the structure: instead of a separate
weft
thread carried on a shuttle, each of the original
warp
threads takes a turn as the working thread, weaving over and under the others from one edge to the centre. For a
chevron

pattern, one thread weaves from the right toward the centre while another weaves from the left toward the centre simultaneously. The two threads cross at the centre, producing the V-shape. Using two contrasting colours (four threads of colour A and four threads of colour B) creates a clear herringbone stripe.

This band uses 8 threads and finishes at approximately 3 cm wide. The length depends on how long you weave: at a comfortable working pace, roughly 2 to 3 cm of band is produced per 10 minutes. A 60 cm band (long enough for a bracelet or bookmark) takes approximately 30 minutes to weave. For a longer sash or strap, cut threads proportionally longer.

items.name: Clipboard or tape for securing the starting knot

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare the threads

Cut 8 lengths of 4-ply cotton, each 2 m long: 4 lengths of colour A and 4 of colour B. Arrange them alternating by colour (A, B, A, B, A, B, A, B) and knot all 8 together at one end with an overhand knot, leaving a 5 cm tail. Secure the knot to a clipboard clip, or tape the tail firmly to a table. The knot needs to hold firm while you weave: it is the point against which you maintain
tension
Step 2: Set up the chevron

Fan the 8 threads out in a row in front of you, holding them taut toward the secured knot. Number the threads 1 to 8, left to right, in your mind: 1 and 2 are colour A, 3 and 4 are colour B, 5 and 6 are colour A, 7 and 8 are colour B (or your chosen alternation). Thread 1 (far left) will weave to the centre; thread 8 (far right) will weave to the centre from the other side. Threads 4 and 5 are the centre pair.
Step 3: Weave from the left

Take thread 1 (far left) in your right hand. Weave it over thread 2, under thread 3, over thread 4, and stop at the centre. The thread now rests between threads 4 and 5. This is one half-row from the left.
Step 4: Weave from the right
Take thread 8 (far right) in your left hand. Weave it over thread 7, under thread 6, over thread 5, and stop at the centre. Thread 8 now rests beside thread 1 at the centre.
Step 5: Cross the centre threads

At the centre, pass thread 1 over thread 8 (or thread 8 over thread 1: choose one direction and keep it consistent for the full band). This crossing is the point of the chevron V. Pull both threads gently outward to seat the crossing and tighten the row. The two threads that crossed are now in each other's original positions.
Step 6: Repeat from the new outermost threads

The thread that just crossed to the right side is now the new rightmost thread. The thread that crossed to the left is now the new leftmost thread. Repeat steps 3 to 5 with the new outermost threads. Each full cycle of left-weave, right-weave, and centre-cross produces one row of the
chevron
pattern. After 5 to 6 rows the pattern becomes visible: a V-shape in the two yarn colours.
Step 7: Maintain tension and selvedges
After each row, press the completed row upward toward the knot using a fingernail or blunt fingertip. This packs the rows and keeps the
selvedge
edges straight. The band will naturally narrow if
tension
is too high, or spread too wide if tension is too low. Aim for a consistent 3 cm width throughout.
Step 8: Finish the band

When the band reaches the intended length, or when the thread ends are too short to weave (less than 10 cm), finish with an overhand knot across all 8 threads. Trim the tail ends to a consistent 3 cm fringe, or divide the ends into two groups of 4 and plait each group into a short braid before knotting. For a bracelet: bring the two knot ends together and connect with a loop-and-button closure using a short loop of thread knotted through the starting knot.

items.problem: Band is narrowing as I weave
items.solution: The tension is too high. Ease the pull on the threads slightly. The threads are interlacing at too steep an angle and pulling the band in. Work at a relaxed, even tension.

Check that the centre crossing is made in the same direction on every row. Reversing the over-under direction at the centre alternating rows produces a diamond rather than a chevron. Consistent direction produces the V-shape.

items.problem: Selvedge is loopy on one side
items.solution: One side is getting a looser outer thread than the other. Press each row up toward the starting knot immediately after finishing it, and check that both outermost threads have the same tension when the crossing is tightened.
Wrap each thread into a butterfly bobbin (a small figure-of-eight wound around two fingers) and secure the bobbin with a slip knot. Unwind a short length at a time as you weave.
